sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: B+ Tree EvoSQL uses a page-based B+ tree called btree2 for all O(log n) key-value indexing. Every primary key index, secondary index, and system catalog tree is a B+ tree instance. Keys are variable-length strings up to 255 bytes. Values are RowID structs that point to heap records.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.text: EvoSQL uses a page-based B+ tree called btree2 for all O(log n) key-value indexing. Every primary key index, secondary index, and system catalog tree is a B+ tree instance. Keys are variable-length strings up to 255 bytes. Values are RowID structs that point to heap records. All tree nodes are stored as 4KB pages managed by the Page Manager. All I/O passes through the Clock Sweep buffer pool. The tree has these key properties. Key format. Length-prefixed string. It uses 1 byte for length plus up to 255 bytes of data. The limit is BT2_MAX_KEY_LEN = 255. Value format. A RowID { page_no: uint32_t, slot_idx: uint16_t }. This is 6 bytes. Numeric-aware comparison. Pure numeric strings are compared by numeric value, so "2" < "10". Non-numeric keys fall back to lexicographic order. Node capacity. About 4076 usable bytes per node. This is 4096 minus the 16-byte PageHeader minus the 4-byte NodeHeader. Deletion strategy. Deletion is lazy. Deleted entries have their RowID set to {0, 0}. The key slot is reused on re-insert of the same key. The BTree2 handle is a single struct that holds the root page number. typedef struct { uint32_t root_page; /* 0 = empty tree */ } BTree2;

sections.heading: Node Structure
sections.text: Every B+ tree node occupies exactly one 4KB page. The first 16 bytes are the standard PageHeader. A 4-byte NodeHeader follows. The key/pointer data area comes after that. The page type field in PageHeader distinguishes internal nodes from leaf nodes. Internal nodes use PAGE_BTREE_INT = 2. Leaf nodes use PAGE_BTREE_LEAF = 3.

sections.heading: NodeHeader
sections.text: typedef struct { uint16_t num_keys; /* number of keys stored in this node */ uint16_t flags; /* reserved, must be 0 */ } NodeHeader; The position within the page is fixed. Offset 0: PageHeader (16 bytes) Offset 16: NodeHeader (4 bytes) Offset 20: Key/pointer data area (4076 bytes)

sections.heading: Leaf Node Layout
sections.text: Leaf nodes store sorted key-RowID pairs. Each entry is a length-prefixed key followed by a 6-byte RowID. Leaf nodes are doubly linked through PageHeader.next_page and PageHeader.prev_page for sequential scans. ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ PageHeader (16B) │ │ page_type = PAGE_BTREE_LEAF │ │ page_id, next_page (→ right leaf), prev_page │ ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ NodeHeader (4B) │ │ num_keys, flags │ ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ Entry 0: [klen:1B][key_data:klen B][RowID:6B] │ │ Entry 1: [klen:1B][key_data:klen B][RowID:6B] │ │ Entry 2: [klen:1B][key_data:klen B][RowID:6B] │ │ ... │ │ (entries sorted by key) │ │ │ │ ← free space → │ └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ The entry overhead per key is 1 (length byte) + key_length + 6 (RowID). This equals 7 plus key_length bytes. For a typical 20-byte key, each entry is 27 bytes. That yields about 150 entries per leaf.

sections.heading: Internal Node Layout
sections.text: Internal nodes store separator keys interleaved with child page pointers. With N keys there are N+1 child pointers. The first child pointer precedes the first key. Each later key is followed by the child pointer for keys greater than or equal to that separator. ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ PageHeader (16B) │ │ page_type = PAGE_BTREE_INT │ │ page_id │ ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ NodeHeader (4B) │ │ num_keys, flags │ ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ child0 (4B) │ │ [key0_len:1B][key0_data:N B] │ │ child1 (4B) │ │ [key1_len:1B][key1_data:N B] │ │ child2 (4B) │ │ ... │ │ childN (4B) │ │ │ │ ← free space → │ └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ The entry overhead per key is 4 (child pointer) + 1 (length byte) + key_length bytes. There is also a trailing 4-byte child pointer at the end. For a typical 20-byte key, the fan-out is about 160 to 170 children per internal node. With this fan-out, a tree of depth 3 can index over 4 million keys.

sections.heading: Leaf Chain (Doubly Linked)
sections.text: All leaf nodes are connected into a doubly linked list through PageHeader.next_page and PageHeader.prev_page. This enables efficient sequential and range scans. The engine does not need to traverse the tree from the root for each next-key lookup. ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐ ... ←────│ Leaf A │←───→│ Leaf B │←───→│ Leaf C │────→ ... │ prev=? │ │prev= A │ │prev= B │ │ next=B │ │next= C │ │ next=0 │ └────────┘ └────────┘ └────────┘

sections.heading: bt2_create · Allocate Root Page
sections.text: int bt2_create(BTree2 *tree); This allocates a single page of type PAGE_BTREE_LEAF through pgm_alloc_page(). It initializes the page as an empty leaf node with num_keys = 0. It writes the page to disk and stores the page number in tree->root_page. It returns 0 on success and -1 on allocation failure. A tree with root_page == 0 is considered empty. bt2_insert() calls bt2_create() automatically if the root is 0, so explicit creation is optional.

sections.heading: bt2_insert · Insert Key/RowID
sections.text: int bt2_insert(BTree2 *tree, const char *key, RowID rid); This inserts a key-RowID mapping into the tree. The key must not exceed BT2_MAX_KEY_LEN, which is 255 bytes. The return values are these. 0. Success. 1. Duplicate key. The key already exists with a non-deleted RowID. -1. Error. This means an allocation failure or a key that is too long. The algorithm runs in these steps. If the tree is empty with root_page == 0, create a root leaf automatically. Call insert_recursive() starting at the root page. The recursive function descends through internal nodes to the correct leaf. At the leaf, it does the following work. Scan entries to find the sorted insertion position. If the key already exists with a deleted RowID of {0, 0}, reuse the slot by overwriting the RowID. Return 0. If the key exists with a live RowID, return 1 for duplicate. If the entry fits in the leaf's free space, shift later entries right and write the entry. Return 0. If the leaf is full, perform a leaf split. See Tree Splitting. If a split propagated up and the root itself split, allocate a new root internal node. The new root holds the separator key and two children. These are the old root and the new sibling.

sections.heading: bt2_search · Exact Key Lookup
sections.text: int bt2_search(BTree2 *tree, const char *key, RowID *out_rid); This searches for an exact key match. It descends from the root through internal nodes using find_leaf(). Then it performs a linear scan of the leaf's entries. The return values are these. 0. Found. The RowID is written to *out_rid. -1. Not found. This also covers a key that exists but is marked deleted with RowID == {0, 0}. The descent logic in find_leaf works as follows. At each internal node it scans separator keys from left to right. It follows child[i] where i is the index of the first separator key greater than the search key. If no separator is greater, it follows the rightmost child.

sections.heading: bt2_delete · Logical Delete
sections.text: int bt2_delete(BTree2 *tree, const char *key); This performs a lazy logical delete. It descends to the leaf that contains the key. It overwrites the entry's RowID with the sentinel value {0, 0}. The key bytes stay in the leaf. The slot is reused if the same key is re-inserted. The return values are these. 0. Success. -1. The key was not found, or it was already deleted. No merging or rebalancing is performed. This keeps the implementation simple. It avoids the complexity of B+ tree node merges. Over time, leaves may accumulate deleted entries. Re-inserts of the same key reclaim those slots.

sections.heading: bt2_update · Update RowID for Existing Key
sections.text: int bt2_update(BTree2 *tree, const char *key, RowID new_rid); This finds the leaf entry for the given key. It overwrites its RowID with new_rid. This is used when a heap record moves to a different page. For example, an UPDATE that changes record size moves the record. The index must then point to the record's updated location. The return values are these. 0. Success. -1. The key was not found. infobt2_update() does not check whether the existing RowID is deleted. It unconditionally overwrites the RowID. This means it can also undelete an entry by writing a valid RowID over a {0, 0} sentinel.

sections.heading: bt2_destroy · Recursive Page Freeing
sections.text: void bt2_destroy(BTree2 *tree); This recursively frees every page in the tree by walking the tree depth-first. At each internal node it recurses into all children before freeing the node's own page through pgm_free_page(). Leaf pages are freed directly. After completion, tree->root_page is set to 0. This is used by DROP TABLE and DROP INDEX to reclaim all storage.

sections.heading: Cursor API
sections.text: The cursor API provides sequential iteration over the B+ tree's leaf chain. It supports full scans and prefix-based range scans. typedef struct { uint32_t cur_page; /* current leaf page (0 = exhausted) */ uint16_t cur_idx; /* current key index within leaf */ } BTree2Cursor;

sections.heading: bt2_cursor_first · Position at Smallest Key
sections.text: int bt2_cursor_first(BTree2 *tree, BTree2Cursor *cur); This descends to the leftmost leaf by always following child[0] at each internal node. It sets the cursor to the first non-deleted entry. If all entries in the first leaf are deleted, it follows next_page to find the first active entry. It returns 0 on success. It returns -1 if the tree is empty or contains only deleted entries.

sections.heading: bt2_cursor_next · Advance to Next Entry
sections.text: int bt2_cursor_next(BTree2Cursor *cur, char *key_out, RowID *rid_out); This returns the current entry's key and RowID, then advances the cursor. The key is null-terminated and at most BT2_MAX_KEY_LEN + 1 bytes. It automatically skips deleted entries with RowID {0, 0}. It follows the leaf chain when the current leaf is exhausted. It returns 0 on success. It returns -1 when no more entries remain. The typical iteration pattern looks like this. BTree2Cursor cur; bt2_cursor_first(&tree, &cur); char key[BT2_MAX_KEY_LEN + 1]; RowID rid; while (bt2_cursor_next(&cur, key, &rid) == 0) { // process key, rid }

sections.heading: bt2_cursor_seek · Position at Key or First Key >= Key
sections.text: int bt2_cursor_seek(BTree2 *tree, const char *key, BTree2Cursor *cur); This descends to the leaf that contains the given key. It uses the same find_leaf() logic as bt2_search. Then it scans the leaf to find the first entry whose key is greater than or equal to the search key. If all keys in the leaf are smaller, it follows next_page. This is the primary mechanism for prefix-based range scans. The system catalog uses composite keys with zero-padded IDs such as "0000000001:tablename". A call to bt2_cursor_seek("0000000001:") positions the cursor at the first entry for table ID 1. Iteration continues with bt2_cursor_next() until the prefix no longer matches. It returns 0 on success. It returns -1 if no key greater than or equal to the search key exists.

sections.heading: Tree Splitting
sections.text: When a leaf or internal node runs out of space during insertion, the node is split into two siblings. A separator key is promoted to the parent. If the parent is also full, the split propagates upward recursively. If the root itself splits, a new root is created. This increases the tree height by one.

sections.heading: Leaf Split
sections.text: The insertion target leaf is full. Used bytes plus entry size is greater than 4076. A temporary oversized buffer is created. The new entry is inserted at its sorted position. The entries are divided at the midpoint with mid = total_keys / 2. Entries [0, mid) stay in the original page. Entries [mid, total) are copied to a newly allocated leaf page. The leaf chain is updated as follows. New leaf's next_page = old leaf's former next_page. New leaf's prev_page = old leaf's page_id. Old leaf's next_page = new leaf's page_id. If a leaf existed after the old leaf, its prev_page is updated to point to the new leaf. The separator key, which is the first key of the new leaf, is promoted to the parent. BEFORE (leaf full, inserting "F"): ┌────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ Leaf: [A][B][C][D][E][G][H] (FULL) │ └────────────────────────────────────────┘ AFTER (split at midpoint, "E" promoted): separator = "E" │ ▼ ┌──── parent receives ("E", ptr→new leaf) ────┐ │ │ ▼ ▼ ┌─────────────────┐ next ┌─────────────────┐ │ [A][B][C][D] │──────────→│ [E][F][G][H] │ │ Old Leaf │←──────────│ New Leaf │ └─────────────────┘ prev └─────────────────┘

sections.heading: Internal Node Split
sections.text: The internal node is full. It cannot fit the new separator key plus child pointer. A temporary oversized buffer is created. The new separator is inserted at its correct position. The entries are divided at the midpoint. The middle key is not kept in either sibling. It is promoted to the parent. Keys [0, mid) and children [0, mid] stay in the original node. The key at index mid is sent up to the parent. Keys [mid+1, total) and children [mid+1, total] go to the new internal node. BEFORE (internal node full, inserting separator "M"): ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ Internal: [ptr0] D [ptr1] G [ptr2] J [ptr3] │ │ K [ptr4] P [ptr5] S [ptr6] (FULL) │ └─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ AFTER (split, "K" promoted to parent): "K" promoted │ ▼ ┌──── parent receives ("K", ptr→right) ────┐ │ │ ▼ ▼ ┌────────────────────────┐ ┌────────────────────────┐ │ [ptr0] D [ptr1] G │ │ [ptr3] M [ptr4] P │ │ [ptr2] J [ptr3] │ │ [ptr5] S [ptr6] │ │ Left (original) │ │ Right (new) │ └────────────────────────┘ └────────────────────────┘

sections.heading: Root Split
sections.text: When the current root splits, bt2_insert() allocates a new internal node as the root. This new root has exactly one separator key. It has two children, which are the old root and its new sibling. It becomes the new tree->root_page. This is the only operation that increases the tree's height. BEFORE: root (leaf or internal, now split) │ ┌─────┴─────┐ old_root new_sibling AFTER: new_root / │ \ child0 "sep" child1 │ │ old_root new_sibling

sections.heading: Sentinel RowID
sections.text: The RowID value {page_no = 0, slot_idx = 0} is the universal deleted sentinel. Page 0 is always the file header and can never contain user data. This value is therefore guaranteed to be invalid as a record pointer. The sentinel behaves like this across the API. OperationBehavior bt2_search()Returns -1 for not found if the matching entry has RowID {0, 0} bt2_delete()Writes {0, 0} over the entry's RowID bt2_insert()If a key exists with RowID {0, 0}, reuses the slot and writes the live RowID bt2_cursor_next()Silently skips entries with RowID {0, 0} bt2_cursor_first()Skips leading deleted entries and advances to the next leaf if needed

sections.heading: Key Comparison
sections.text: The key_cmp() function implements numeric-aware comparison. It works in these steps. Attempt to parse both keys as floating-point numbers with strtod. If both parse successfully with no trailing characters, compare numerically. Otherwise, fall back to lexicographic comparison. This uses memcmp with length tie-breaking. This ensures that numeric primary keys sort naturally, so "2" < "10" rather than "10" < "2". String keys retain standard dictionary order.

sections.heading: Max Key Length
sections.text: The maximum key length is 255 bytes through BT2_MAX_KEY_LEN. This is constrained by the 1-byte length prefix used in the node entry format. bt2_insert() rejects keys longer than this limit with a return value of -1. In practice, keys are much shorter. Primary key indexes. The string representation of column values. This is typically 5 to 40 bytes. System catalog composite keys. Zero-padded 10-digit IDs with a : separator. For example "0000000001:users" is 17 bytes. Secondary index keys. The column value concatenated with the primary key for uniqueness.

sections.heading: Usage
sections.text: The B+ tree is the sole indexing structure in EvoSQL. Every index is a BTree2 instance. This holds for user tables and for internal metadata.

sections.heading: System Catalog
sections.text: The system catalog uses 8 B+ trees for all metadata. They are stored in FileHeader.catalog_roots[]. CatalogIDTreePurpose CAT_SYS_DATABASESDatabasesDatabase name to database record CAT_SYS_SCHEMASSchemasdb_id:schema_name to schema record CAT_SYS_TABLESTablesschema_id:table_name to table record CAT_SYS_COLUMNSColumnstable_id:ordinal to column record CAT_SYS_INDEXESIndexestable_id:index_name to index record CAT_SYS_CONSTRAINTSConstraintstable_id:constraint_name to constraint record CAT_SYS_USERSUsersUsername to user record CAT_SYS_GRANTSGrantsGrant key to privilege record Catalog trees use composite keys with zero-padded IDs and : separators. bt2_cursor_seek() with a prefix like "0000000005:" enables efficient iteration over all entries that belong to a specific parent. For example it can iterate over all columns of table 5.

sections.heading: Primary Key Indexes
sections.text: Every table with a primary key has an associated B+ tree. The key is the string representation of the primary key column values. The RowID points to the heap page and slot where the full record is stored. INSERT checks for duplicates through bt2_search() before inserting. SELECT ... WHERE pk = value uses bt2_search() for O(log n) point lookups. See Table API and DML.

sections.heading: Secondary Indexes
sections.text: Secondary indexes created through CREATE INDEX are also B+ trees. Index.c populates the tree by scanning the table's heap pages with tapi_scan and inserting one entry per row.

It describes the C code in src/backend. This wiki covers version . infoThis wiki is about the engine only. It does not cover the agent, the SDKs, or the connectors. For SQL usage see the reference manual. For the client libraries see the developer portal.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: Page Manager and Slotted Pages EvoSQL keeps all data in one global binary file made of fixed-size 4KB pages. The Page Manager owns this file and handles startup, page allocation, page free, and raw I/O. Slotted Pages store variable-length records inside a single data page using a slot directory layout. The global file holds every database, table, index, and the system catalog. The Page Manager lives in page_mgr.h and page_mgr.c. It owns the file. It handles initialization, page allocation and free, and raw I/O. Slotted Pages live in slotted.h and slotted.c. They provide variable-length record storage inside individual data pages. All disk I/O flows through the Clock Sweep buffer pool in buffer_pool.c. The page manager never calls read() or write() directly. It uses bp_read() and bp_write() instead. Source files: evolution/db/page_mgr.h, evolution/db/page_mgr.c evolution/db/slotted.h, evolution/db/slotted.c tests/test_page_mgr.c (unit tests)

sections.heading: File Structure
sections.text: The global storage file is a sequence of 4096-byte pages. Page 0 is always the File Header. All remaining pages are available for allocation. When the server starts, pgm_init() opens the existing file or creates a fresh one. When it opens an existing file, it validates the magic number and the page size. When it creates a fresh file, it makes 64 pages, which is 256KB. Page 0 Page 1 Page 2 Page N-1 ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐ │FileHeader│ │ Data / │ │ Data / │ ... │ Data / │ │ (4096 B) │ │ Free / │ │ Free / │ │ Free / │ │ │ │ BTree │ │ BTree │ │ BTree │ └──────────┘ └──────────┘ └──────────┘ └──────────┘

sections.heading: Constants
sections.text: ConstantValueDescription EVO_MAGIC0x45564F44Magic number (ASCII "EVOD") EVO_FILE_VERSION1File format version EVO_PAGE_SIZE4096Page size in bytes PAGE_HEADER_SIZE16Common page header size PAGE_USABLE4080Usable bytes per page (EVO_PAGE_SIZE - PAGE_HEADER_SIZE) PGM_INITIAL_PAGES64Pages in a newly created file (256KB) PGM_GROW_MIN16Minimum number of pages added when growing the file

sections.heading: Common Page Header
sections.text: Every page in the file begins with a 16-byte header. typedef struct { uint8_t page_type; /* PageType enum */ uint8_t flags; /* reserved, must be 0 */ uint16_t free_space; /* bytes of free space on this page */ uint32_t page_id; /* this page's own number (self-check) */ uint32_t next_page; /* free list chain or B+ leaf chain */ uint32_t prev_page; /* B+ tree leaf backward pointer */ } PageHeader; /* 16 bytes */ The page_type field uses one of five values. typedef enum { PAGE_FREE = 0, /* available for allocation */ PAGE_DATA = 1, /* slotted page for row storage */ PAGE_BTREE_INT = 2, /* B+ tree internal node */ PAGE_BTREE_LEAF = 3, /* B+ tree leaf node */ PAGE_OVERFLOW = 4 /* overflow for large records */ } PageType; The next_page field has two jobs. On free pages it chains into the free list. On B+ tree leaf pages it links to the next leaf in key order. The prev_page field links B+ tree leaves backward.

sections.heading: File Header (Page 0)
sections.text: Page 0 holds the global file header. It takes up an entire page. A reserved array pads it to fill exactly EVO_PAGE_SIZE bytes. #define CATALOG_ROOT_SLOTS 16 typedef struct { uint32_t magic; /* EVO_MAGIC */ uint16_t version; /* EVO_FILE_VERSION */ uint16_t page_size; /* EVO_PAGE_SIZE (4096) */ uint32_t total_pages; /* total pages in file */ uint32_t free_list_head; /* first free page (0 = none) */ uint32_t catalog_roots[CATALOG_ROOT_SLOTS]; /* root pages for system catalog trees */ uint32_t next_table_id; /* auto-increment counter */ uint32_t next_schema_id; /* auto-increment counter */ uint32_t next_db_id; /* auto-increment counter */ uint8_t reserved[EVO_PAGE_SIZE - 92]; /* pad to full page */ } FileHeader; The fixed fields sum to exactly 92 bytes. The math is 4 + 2 + 2 + 4 + 4 + (16 * 4) + 4 + 4 + 4 = 92. The reserved array takes the remaining bytes. That is 4096 - 92 = 4004 bytes. Catalog root slots store the root page number of each system catalog B+ tree. The engine defines eight catalog IDs. typedef enum { CAT_SYS_DATABASES = 0, CAT_SYS_SCHEMAS = 1, CAT_SYS_TABLES = 2, CAT_SYS_COLUMNS = 3, CAT_SYS_INDEXES = 4, CAT_SYS_CONSTRAINTS = 5, CAT_SYS_USERS = 6, CAT_SYS_GRANTS = 7, CAT_MAX = 8 } CatalogID; Slots 8 through 15 are reserved for future use. Auto-increment counters give globally unique IDs. They are next_table_id, next_schema_id, and next_db_id. They start at 1 in a fresh file. They are incremented atomically through pgm_next_id().

sections.heading: Initialization
sections.text: pgm_init(filepath) is called once at server startup. It runs after the buffer pool has been initialized with bp_init(). It follows this logic. If the file already exists, open it and read page 0. Validate that magic == EVO_MAGIC and page_size == EVO_PAGE_SIZE. If validation fails, return an error. If the file does not exist (ENOENT), create it with O_RDWR | O_CREAT | O_TRUNC. Extend it to PGM_INITIAL_PAGES * EVO_PAGE_SIZE bytes through ftruncate(). Write the initial FileHeader to page 0. Then chain pages 1 through PGM_INITIAL_PAGES - 1 into the free list. If it is already initialized, this is a re-entrant call. Return immediately. pgm_shutdown() flushes any dirty file header. It calls bp_flush_all() to write all dirty buffer pool pages to disk. Then it closes the file descriptor.

sections.heading: Page Allocation
sections.text: pgm_alloc_page(page_type) pops a page from the head of the free list. If free_list_head == 0, the list is empty. Grow the file first. Read the page at free_list_head. Extract next_page from its header. This becomes the new free_list_head. Zero-fill the page buffer. Set page_type to the requested type. Write it back through the buffer pool. Flush the updated file header. Return the allocated page number. A return value of 0 indicates an error. Page 0 is never allocatable.

sections.heading: Page Deallocation
sections.text: pgm_free_page(page_no) prepends the page to the free list. Refuse to free page 0. It is always the file header. Zero-fill the page. Set page_type = PAGE_FREE. Set next_page to the current free_list_head. Write the page through the buffer pool. Update free_list_head to point to this page. Flush the file header. The free list is a singly-linked chain through PageHeader.next_page. FileHeader Page 7 Page 3 Page 12 ┌────────────┐ ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐ │free_list │ │PAGE_FREE │ │PAGE_FREE │ │PAGE_FREE │ │ _head = 7 ─┼──────────>│next = 3 ─┼────>│next = 12─┼────>│next = 0 │ └────────────┘ └──────────┘ └──────────┘ └──────────┘ (end of list)

sections.heading: File Growth
sections.text: When pgm_alloc_page() finds the free list empty, the internal grow_file() function extends the file. Compute the growth amount. It is 50% of the current total_pages. It is at least PGM_GROW_MIN, which is 16 pages. Call ftruncate() to extend the file to the new size. Chain all newly created pages into the free list through chain_free_pages(). Each page is zero-filled with page_type = PAGE_FREE and linked to the next. The last page in the batch points to the previous free_list_head. Update total_pages and free_list_head in the file header.

sections.heading: Raw Page I/O
sections.text: int pgm_read_page(uint32_t page_no, void *buf); int pgm_write_page(uint32_t page_no, const void *buf); These are thin wrappers over bp_read() and bp_write(). They compute the byte offset as page_no * EVO_PAGE_SIZE. They transfer a full 4096-byte page through the buffer pool. They return 0 on success and -1 on error. The buffer must be at least EVO_PAGE_SIZE bytes. pgm_flush() writes the file header if it is dirty. It then calls bp_flush_all() to force all cached pages to disk.

sections.heading: Catalog Root Management
sections.text: uint32_t pgm_get_catalog_root(CatalogID id); void pgm_set_catalog_root(CatalogID id, uint32_t page_no); These read and write entries in the catalog_roots[] array of the file header. A system catalog B+ tree is created for the first time. Its root page number is stored here. This lets the engine find it on later startups. Setting a root flushes the header right away. The system catalog itself is described on the System Catalog page.

sections.heading: Auto-Increment IDs
sections.text: uint32_t pgm_next_id(int id_type); /* 0 = table, 1 = schema, 2 = database */ This returns the current value and increments the counter atomically. The file header is flushed after each call. This makes the change durable.

sections.heading: Thread Safety
sections.text: A single pthread_mutex_t named g_pgm_lock serializes all page manager operations. This covers allocation, free, catalog root access, and ID generation. Raw I/O does not acquire the page manager mutex. pgm_read_page and pgm_write_page rely on the buffer pool's own mutex for thread safety.

sections.heading: Global State
sections.text: The page manager is a singleton. It maintains four pieces of state. g_global_fd is the file descriptor for the global storage file. g_header is the in-memory copy of the FileHeader, which is page 0. g_header_dirty is a flag for unsaved header changes. g_pgm_lock is the mutex for thread safety. The file descriptor is exposed through pgm_get_fd(). This is for callers that need direct buffer pool access.

sections.heading: Complete API Summary
sections.text: /* Lifecycle */ int pgm_init(const char *filepath); void pgm_shutdown(void); /* Page allocation */ uint32_t pgm_alloc_page(uint8_t page_type); void pgm_free_page(uint32_t page_no); /* Raw page I/O */ int pgm_read_page(uint32_t page_no, void *buf); int pgm_write_page(uint32_t page_no, const void *buf); void pgm_flush(void); /* Catalog roots */ uint32_t pgm_get_catalog_root(CatalogID id); void pgm_set_catalog_root(CatalogID id, uint32_t page_no); /* Auto-increment IDs */ uint32_t pgm_next_id(int id_type); /* Utility */ int pgm_get_fd(void);

sections.heading: Slotted Pages
sections.text: Slotted pages manage variable-length records inside a single 4KB page. The design follows a classic database textbook layout. A slot directory grows forward from the top of the page. Record data grows backward from the bottom. This gives stable record addressing through slot indices even as records are inserted and deleted.

sections.heading: Page Layout
sections.text: Byte 0 Byte 4095 ┌──────────────┬───────────────┬────────┬────────┬─ ─┬──────────┐ │ PageHeader │ SlottedHeader │ Slot 0 │ Slot 1 │ .. │ │ │ (16 B) │ (6 B) │ (4 B) │ (4 B) │ │ │ ├──────────────┴───────────────┴────────┴────────┴─ ─┤ │ │ │ │ │ FREE SPACE │ │ │ │ │ │ <── free_offset free_end ──> │ │ ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ │ │ Record 1 │ Record 0 │ │ │ │ (var) │ (var) │ │ └──────────────┴──────────┴──────────┴────────────────────────────┘ Slot directory grows FORWARD (toward higher offsets) ──────> Record area grows BACKWARD (toward lower offsets) <────── When a slotted page is initialized through slot_page_init(), three values are set. free_offset = PAGE_HEADER_SIZE + SLOTTED_HEADER_SIZE = byte 22, just past the slotted header. free_end = EVO_PAGE_SIZE = byte 4096, the end of the page. Free space = 4096 - 22 = 4074 bytes, before any slots are allocated. The free space is the gap between free_offset and free_end. The free_offset marks the end of the slot directory. The free_end marks the start of the record area. As slots are added, free_offset advances forward by 4 bytes each. As records are placed, free_end retreats backward by the record size.

sections.heading: SlottedHeader
sections.text: typedef struct { uint16_t num_slots; /* total slot entries (including deleted) */ uint16_t free_offset; /* byte offset of first free byte after slot directory */ uint16_t free_end; /* byte offset where next record will be placed */ } SlottedHeader; /* 6 bytes */ num_slots counts all slots ever created on this page. This includes slots marked as deleted. It only grows. It never shrinks. free_offset tracks the end of the slot directory. It equals PAGE_HEADER_SIZE + SLOTTED_HEADER_SIZE + (num_slots * SLOT_ENTRY_SIZE) when no deleted slots have been reused. free_end tracks the beginning of the record region. Records are placed just below this offset. Then free_end is decremented.

sections.heading: Slot Entry
sections.text: typedef struct { uint16_t offset; /* byte offset of record within page (0 = deleted) */ uint16_t length; /* record length in bytes */ } SlotEntry; /* 4 bytes */ A slot with offset == 0 is treated as deleted or empty. This works because byte 0 is taken by the PageHeader. No valid record can start there. Slot entries are stored one after another starting at byte 22. That is after the PageHeader and the SlottedHeader. Slot i is at byte offset 22 + (i * 4).

sections.heading: RowID
sections.text: typedef struct { uint32_t page_no; uint16_t slot_idx; } RowID; /* 6 bytes */ A RowID uniquely identifies any record in the storage file. The pair of page number and slot index is stable across compaction. Slot indices are never renumbered. A RowID with page_no == 0 is the "deleted" sentinel. Page 0 is always the file header and never stores records.

sections.heading: Maximum Record Size
sections.text: #define SLOT_MAX_RECORD (EVO_PAGE_SIZE - PAGE_HEADER_SIZE - SLOTTED_HEADER_SIZE - SLOT_ENTRY_SIZE) This evaluates to 4096 - 16 - 6 - 4 = 4070 bytes. A record of this size would use the entire page. That is one slot entry plus the record data. It leaves zero free space.

sections.heading: Record Operations
sections.text: Insert (slot_insert) int slot_insert(void *page_buf, const void *record, uint16_t rec_len); It returns the slot index on success. It returns -1 if the record does not fit. The algorithm runs as follows. Reject zero-length records. Reject records larger than SLOT_MAX_RECORD. Scan the slot directory for a deleted slot. A deleted slot has offset == 0 and length == 0. If one is found, reuse it. No new slot overhead is needed. Calculate the space required. That is rec_len bytes for the record. Add 4 bytes for a new slot entry if a deleted slot is not being reused. If the contiguous free gap (free_end - free_offset) is too small, try slot_compact() to defragment the page. If it is still not enough, return -1. Place the record at free_end - rec_len and update free_end. Write the slot entry. It is either the reused slot or a newly appended entry in the directory. Update PageHeader.free_space. Read (slot_read) int slot_read(const void *page_buf, uint16_t slot_idx, void *out_buf, uint16_t out_size); It returns the actual record length on success. It returns -1 if the slot index is out of range or the slot is deleted. It copies at most out_size bytes into out_buf. If the record is larger than out_size, it is truncated. The full length is still returned. The caller can then allocate a larger buffer and retry. Delete (slot_delete) int slot_delete(void *page_buf, uint16_t slot_idx); It returns 0 on success. It returns -1 if the slot is already deleted or out of range. Check if the record being deleted is the physically last record. That is when se->offset == free_end. If so, advance free_end by se->length to reclaim the space right away. Mark the slot as deleted. Set offset = 0 and length = 0. Update PageHeader.free_space. Records that are not physically last leave a gap. This is fragmentation. slot_compact() reclaims this space. Update (slot_update) int slot_update(void *page_buf, uint16_t slot_idx, const void *new_record, uint16_t new_len); It returns 0 on success. It returns -1 if the record does not fit. If new_len <= old_length, the record is overwritten in place. If it is shorter than the original, the tail bytes become wasted space. Compaction can reclaim them. The slot's length is updated to new_len. If new_len > old_length, the function returns -1. The caller must do a delete-then-insert. This may land on a different page if the current page lacks space. The table API layer uses this overflow handling pattern. See Table API and DML.

sections.heading: Free Space Management
sections.text: Contiguous Free Space slot_free_space() returns the contiguous gap between the end of the slot directory and the start of the record area. uint16_t slot_free_space(const void *page_buf); // Returns: free_end - free_offset (or 0 if they've crossed) This is the maximum amount of data that can be stored without compaction. Inserting a record consumes record bytes. It may also consume 4 bytes for a new slot entry. Fragmentation Fragmentation happens in two cases. A record in the middle of the record area is deleted. This leaves a gap. A record is updated to a shorter length. This leaves unused tail bytes. These gaps reduce the effective capacity of the page. They do not reduce the value returned by slot_free_space(). That value only measures the contiguous gap. Compaction reclaims this space. Compaction (slot_compact) void slot_compact(void *page_buf); It defragments the record area. It repacks all active records toward the end of the page and removes gaps. Allocate a temporary buffer. Walk all slots in order. For each active slot that is not deleted, copy its record to the temporary buffer. Pack records from the end of the page backward. Copy the compacted records back into the page buffer. Update each slot's offset to its new position. Update free_end to reflect the new start of the record area. infoCompaction preserves slot indices. It never renumbers slots. It never removes deleted slot entries from the directory. This matters because external references point to specific slot indices. Those references include B+ tree leaf entries and RowIDs. Compaction is triggered automatically by slot_insert(). This happens when the contiguous free gap is too small but the page has enough total free space once fragmented gaps are counted.

sections.heading: Active Record Count
sections.text: uint16_t slot_active_count(const void *page_buf); It returns the number of slots that are not deleted. It scans the directory and counts entries where offset != 0.

sections.heading: Caller Responsibilities
sections.text: All slotted page functions work on an in-memory page buffer. They do not read from or write to disk. The caller must do two things. Read the page into a buffer through pgm_read_page() before operating on it. Call pgm_write_page() after modifying the page. This persists changes through the buffer pool. This design gives callers full control over I/O batching. It also allows several modifications to a page before a single write.

sections.heading: Detailed Layout Example
sections.text: Three records are inserted. R0 is 100 bytes. R1 is 50 bytes. R2 is 200 bytes. Then R1 is deleted. Byte: 0 16 22 26 30 34 3746 3846 3946 4096 ┌──────────┬────┬────┬────┬────┬─────────────────────────────┬─────┬─────┬─────┐ │PageHeader│Slot│ S0 │ S1 │ S2 │ FREE SPACE │ R2 │(gap)│ R0 │ │ (16 B) │Hdr │off │DEL │off │ │200B │ 50B │100B │ │ │6 B │len │0,0 │len │ │ │ │ │ └──────────┴────┴────┴────┴────┴─────────────────────────────┴─────┴─────┴─────┘ │ ↑ ↑ │ free_offset = 34 free_end = 3746 │ num_slots = 3 S0: offset=3946, length=100 (points to R0) S1: offset=0, length=0 (deleted) S2: offset=3746, length=200 (points to R2) The 50-byte gap at 3846-3896 is fragmented space from the deleted R1. It will be reclaimed when slot_compact() runs. After compaction the page looks like this. Byte: 0 16 22 26 30 34 3796 3996 4096 ┌──────────┬────┬────┬────┬────┬──────────────────────────────────┬─────┬─────┐ │PageHeader│Slot│ S0 │ S1 │ S2 │ FREE SPACE │ R2 │ R0 │ │ (16 B) │Hdr │off │DEL │off │ │200B │100B │ │ │6 B │len │0,0 │len │ │ │ │ └──────────┴────┴────┴────┴────┴──────────────────────────────────┴─────┴─────┘ │ ↑ ↑ │ free_offset = 34 free_end = 3796 │ num_slots = 3 (unchanged) S0: offset=3996, length=100 (updated to new position) S1: offset=0, length=0 (still deleted -- slot not removed) S2: offset=3796, length=200 (updated to new position) The 50-byte gap is gone. Free space increased from 3712 to 3762 bytes.
