sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: System Catalog The system catalog stores all database metadata inside the unified storage file. Eight B+ trees map keys to records, and the records live as semicolon-delimited strings on slotted pages. This page describes the catalog trees, the descriptor structs, the key design, the serialization format, and the full catalog API. Source files. evolution/db/catalog_internal.h, evolution/db/catalog_internal.c. Tests. tests/test_catalog.c with 30 tests.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.text: The system catalog stores all database metadata inside the unified storage file evosql.db. This metadata covers databases, schemas, tables, columns, indexes, constraints, user accounts, and privilege grants. There are no external metadata files. Every piece of metadata lives in one of eight B+ trees. The actual records are stored as semicolon-delimited strings on slotted pages. The B+ tree maps a key to a RowID. A RowID is a page number plus a slot index. The RowID points to the record on a slotted page. ┌─────────────────────────┐ │ FileHeader │ │ (Page 0 of evosql.db) │ │ │ │ catalog_roots[0..7] │ ← root page number of each B+ tree │ next_table_id │ │ next_schema_id │ │ next_db_id │ └────────┬────────────────┘ │ ┌────────────────┼────────────────┐ ▼ ▼ ▼ B+ Tree 0 B+ Tree 1 ... B+ Tree 7 (databases) (schemas) (grants) │ │ │ ▼ ▼ ▼ Slotted Pages Slotted Pages Slotted Pages (records) (records) (records) All catalog I/O flows through the buffer pool and the page manager. There is no direct file access. See the Unified Storage Overview and the Page Manager and Slotted Pages pages for the layers underneath.

sections.heading: The Eight Catalog Trees
sections.text: Each catalog tree is identified by a CatalogID constant defined in page_mgr.h. The root page number of every tree is persisted in the FileHeader.catalog_roots[] array on page 0. ConstantIDPurposeB+ Tree Key CAT_SYS_DATABASES0Database registrydb_name CAT_SYS_SCHEMAS1Schema registrydb_id:schema_name CAT_SYS_TABLES2Table descriptorsschema_id:table_name CAT_SYS_COLUMNS3Column definitionstable_id:col_ordinal CAT_SYS_INDEXES4Secondary index metadatatable_id:index_name CAT_SYS_CONSTRAINTS5CHECK, UNIQUE, FK, PK constraintstable_id:ordinal CAT_SYS_USERS6User accounts with PBKDF2-SHA256 hashed passwordsusername CAT_SYS_GRANTS7Privilege grants with DATABASE, SCHEMA, or TABLE scopeusername:scope_type:scope_name The CatalogID enum and the CATALOG_ROOT_SLOTS constant are defined in page_mgr.h. There are 16 slots and 8 are used. typedef enum { CAT_SYS_DATABASES = 0, CAT_SYS_SCHEMAS = 1, CAT_SYS_TABLES = 2, CAT_SYS_COLUMNS = 3, CAT_SYS_INDEXES = 4, CAT_SYS_CONSTRAINTS = 5, CAT_SYS_USERS = 6, CAT_SYS_GRANTS = 7, CAT_MAX = 8 } CatalogID;

sections.heading: Descriptor Structs
sections.text: Each catalog tree has a matching C struct that represents one record. These structs are defined in catalog_internal.h.

sections.heading: DatabaseDesc
sections.text: typedef struct { uint32_t db_id; char db_name[CAT_MAX_NAME_LEN]; // 128 } DatabaseDesc; The B+ tree key is the database name itself, for example "evosql". The record stores only the db_id. The name is recovered from the key.

sections.heading: SchemaDesc
sections.text: typedef struct { uint32_t schema_id; uint32_t db_id; char schema_name[CAT_MAX_NAME_LEN]; } SchemaDesc; Key format is "%010u:%s". It is the zero-padded db_id, then a colon, then the schema name.

sections.heading: TableDesc
sections.text: typedef struct { uint32_t table_id; uint32_t schema_id; char table_name[CAT_MAX_NAME_LEN]; uint32_t pk_root_page; // root page of the table's primary-key B+ tree uint32_t heap_page; // first slotted page for row storage (0 = lazy alloc) int num_columns; int pad_size; int auto_inc_col; // column ordinal, or -1 if none int auto_inc_counter; int auto_inc_step; } TableDesc; Key format is "%010u:%s". It is the zero-padded schema_id, then a colon, then the table name. When a table is created, cat_create_table also creates a dedicated B+ tree for the table's primary index. It records its root page in pk_root_page. The heap_page field starts at 0. It is set on the first row insertion through cat_update_heap_page.

sections.heading: ColumnDesc
sections.text: typedef struct { uint32_t table_id; int col_ordinal; char col_name[CAT_MAX_NAME_LEN]; int type_code; // typeVal / 10000 = type family, % 10000 = size int is_not_null; int is_unique; int is_pk; char default_val[CAT_MAX_DEFAULT_LEN]; // 256 } ColumnDesc; Key format is "%010u:%010d". It is the zero-padded table_id, then a colon, then the zero-padded col_ordinal. Both parts are numeric and zero-padded. This ensures columns are returned in ordinal order during prefix scans.

sections.heading: IndexDesc
sections.text: typedef struct { uint32_t table_id; char index_name[CAT_MAX_NAME_LEN]; uint32_t root_page; char col_list[CAT_MAX_NAME_LEN]; // comma-separated column names char index_type; // 'P' primary, 'U' unique, 'N' normal } IndexDesc; Key format is "%010u:%s". It is the zero-padded table_id, then a colon, then the index name.

sections.heading: ConstraintDesc
sections.text: typedef struct { uint32_t table_id; int ordinal; char constraint_type; // 'C' check, 'F' foreign key, 'P' primary, 'U' unique char definition[1024]; uint32_t ref_table_id; // for FK, 0 otherwise char ref_columns[CAT_MAX_NAME_LEN]; } ConstraintDesc; Key format is "%010u:%010d". It is the zero-padded table_id, then a colon, then the zero-padded ordinal. The ordinal is assigned from a global counter named g_constraint_ordinal. That counter increments monotonically across all tables.

sections.heading: UserDesc
sections.text: typedef struct { char username[CAT_MAX_NAME_LEN]; char password_hash[CAT_MAX_HASH_LEN]; // 512 } UserDesc; The B+ tree key is the username. The record stores the PBKDF2-SHA256 password hash. The username is recovered from the key.

sections.heading: GrantDesc
sections.text: typedef struct { char username[CAT_MAX_NAME_LEN]; int scope_type; // 1=DATABASE, 2=SCHEMA, 3=TABLE char scope_name[CAT_MAX_NAME_LEN]; char privileges[CAT_MAX_NAME_LEN]; // comma-separated or "ALL" int grant_option; } GrantDesc; Key format is "%s:%d:%s". It is the username, then the scope type integer, then the scope name. This enables a prefix scan by "username:" to find all grants for a given user.

sections.heading: Composite Key Design
sections.text: B+ tree keys are variable-length strings up to 255 bytes. The limit is CAT_MAX_KEY_LEN, which equals BT2_MAX_KEY_LEN. Composite keys use : as the separator. Numeric ID components are zero-padded to 10 digits so that lexicographic comparison preserves numeric ordering. "0000000001:users" schema 1, table "users" "0000000003:0000000002" table 3, column ordinal 2 "admin:1:evosql" user "admin", scope DATABASE(1), database "evosql" The B+ tree comparator performs lexicographic comparison on all keys. The keys contain : so they are treated as strings, not numbers. Zero-padding guarantees that "0000000002" sorts before "0000000010". This encoding also enables efficient prefix scans. To list all columns of table 5, seek to "0000000005:" and iterate while keys still begin with that prefix. Every cat_list_* function uses this pattern. See the B+ Tree page for the cursor and comparator details.

sections.heading: Serialization Format
sections.text: Records are stored as semicolon-delimited strings on slotted pages. This matches the project-wide convention for row storage. Each descriptor struct has a serialize_* and deserialize_* function pair. For example, a TableDesc serializes to this string. "1;1;users;42;0;3;256;-1;0;0" │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ └─ auto_inc_step │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ └─── auto_inc_counter │ │ │ │ │ │ │ └────── auto_inc_col (-1 = none) │ │ │ │ │ │ └────────── pad_size │ │ │ │ │ └──────────── num_columns │ │ │ │ └────────────── heap_page │ │ │ └───────────────── pk_root_page │ │ └─────────────────────── table_name │ └───────────────────────── schema_id └─────────────────────────── table_id Field extraction uses the internal next_field() helper. It reads characters up to the next ; or the end of the string. For some descriptors, the key carries data that the record omits. DatabaseDesc. The record contains only db_id. The name comes from the B+ tree key. UserDesc. The record contains only the password hash. The username comes from the key. Maximum record size is CAT_MAX_RECORD_LEN, which is 2048 bytes.

sections.heading: ID Generation
sections.text: Object IDs for databases, schemas, and tables are auto-incrementing integers stored in the FileHeader. typedef struct { // ... uint32_t next_table_id; // auto-increment for table IDs uint32_t next_schema_id; // auto-increment for schema IDs uint32_t next_db_id; // auto-increment for database IDs // ... } FileHeader; The pgm_next_id(int id_type) function atomically reads and increments the right counter. id_typeCounterUsed by 0next_table_idcat_create_table 1next_schema_idcat_create_schema 2next_db_idcat_create_database IDs start at 1. The counter initializes to 1 in a fresh file header. IDs are never reused after deletion.

sections.heading: Startup sequence
sections.text: pgm_init("evosql.db") opens or creates the storage file. It initializes the page manager and the buffer pool. cat_init() initializes the system catalog.

sections.heading: First run with an empty file
sections.text: cat_init() detects that catalog_roots[CAT_SYS_DATABASES] is 0. This means there is no existing catalog. It then performs a full bootstrap. Creates 8 B+ trees through bt2_create(). Stores each tree's root page number in FileHeader.catalog_roots[] through pgm_set_catalog_root(). Inserts the default database "evosql". It is assigned db_id = 1. Inserts the default schema "default" under database 1. It is assigned schema_id = 1. Inserts the default user "admin" with an empty password hash. The server's authentication layer sets the real PBKDF2-SHA256 hash on first startup.

sections.heading: Subsequent runs
sections.text: cat_init() loads the 8 root page numbers from FileHeader.catalog_roots[] into the in-memory BTree2 handles. Data pages for each catalog tree are lazily allocated on the first insert after restart. This costs at most 8 pages, which is 32 KB of overhead.

sections.heading: Shutdown
sections.text: cat_shutdown() calls pgm_flush() to write all dirty buffer pool pages to disk. It then resets all in-memory state.

sections.heading: Lifecycle
sections.text: int cat_init(void); // Bootstrap or load catalog; call after pgm_init() void cat_shutdown(void); // Flush dirty pages, reset state

sections.heading: Database Operations
sections.text: int cat_create_database(const char *name); // Returns db_id (>0) on success, -1 if name already exists. int cat_find_database(const char *name, DatabaseDesc *out); // Returns 0 on success, -1 if not found. Pass NULL for out to check existence. int cat_drop_database(const char *name); // Returns 0 on success, -1 if not found. int cat_list_databases(DatabaseDesc *out, int max); // Returns count of databases written to out[]. Full tree scan.

sections.heading: Schema Operations
sections.text: int cat_create_schema(uint32_t db_id, const char *name); // Returns schema_id (>0) on success, -1 if duplicate. int cat_find_schema(uint32_t db_id, const char *name, SchemaDesc *out); int cat_drop_schema(uint32_t db_id, const char *name); int cat_list_schemas(uint32_t db_id, SchemaDesc *out, int max); // Prefix scan on db_id. Returns only schemas belonging to the given database.

sections.heading: Table Operations
sections.text: int cat_create_table(uint32_t schema_id, const char *name, const ColumnDesc *cols, int ncols, int pad_size, int auto_inc_col, int auto_inc_start, int auto_inc_step); // Creates table descriptor, a primary-key B+ tree, and all column records. // Returns table_id (>0) on success, -1 on duplicate or error. int cat_find_table(uint32_t schema_id, const char *name, TableDesc *out); int cat_drop_table(uint32_t table_id); // Cascading delete: removes the table record plus all associated columns, // indexes, and constraints from the catalog. The caller is responsible for // destroying the PK B+ tree and freeing heap pages. int cat_update_auto_inc(uint32_t table_id, const char *table_name, uint32_t schema_id, int new_counter); // Updates auto_inc_counter in the table record. Attempts in-place slotted // page update; falls back to delete + re-insert if the record does not fit. int cat_update_heap_page(uint32_t table_id, const char *table_name, uint32_t schema_id, uint32_t heap_page); // Sets the heap_page field (called on first row insertion). int cat_list_tables(uint32_t schema_id, TableDesc *out, int max); // Prefix scan on schema_id.

sections.heading: Column Operations
sections.text: int cat_find_columns(uint32_t table_id, ColumnDesc *out, int max); // Prefix scan on table_id. Returns columns in ordinal order (guaranteed by // zero-padded ordinal in the B+ tree key). Returns count. Columns are created as part of cat_create_table. They are deleted as part of cat_drop_table. There is no standalone column creation API.

sections.heading: Index Operations
sections.text: int cat_create_index(uint32_t table_id, const char *name, uint32_t root_page, const char *col_list, char index_type); // index_type: 'P' primary, 'U' unique, 'N' normal. int cat_find_index(uint32_t table_id, const char *name, IndexDesc *out); // Direct key lookup (requires table_id). int cat_find_index_by_name(const char *name, IndexDesc *out); // Full tree scan. Finds index by name alone, regardless of table. int cat_list_indexes(uint32_t table_id, IndexDesc *out, int max); // Prefix scan on table_id. int cat_drop_index(uint32_t table_id, const char *name); int cat_update_index_root(uint32_t table_id, const char *name, uint32_t new_root_page); // Updates root_page after B+ tree splits or merges change the root.

sections.heading: Constraint Operations
sections.text: int cat_add_constraint(uint32_t table_id, char type, const char *definition, uint32_t ref_table_id, const char *ref_columns); // type: 'C' check, 'F' foreign key, 'P' primary key, 'U' unique. // ref_table_id and ref_columns are used only for FK constraints (pass 0 and "" otherwise). // Ordinal is assigned from a global counter. int cat_list_constraints(uint32_t table_id, ConstraintDesc *out, int max); // Prefix scan on table_id.

sections.heading: User Operations
sections.text: int cat_create_user(const char *username, const char *password_hash); int cat_find_user(const char *username, UserDesc *out); int cat_update_user(const char *username, const char *new_hash); int cat_drop_user(const char *username); int cat_list_users(UserDesc *out, int max); // Full tree scan.

sections.heading: Grant Operations
sections.text: int cat_create_grant(const char *username, int scope_type, const char *scope_name, const char *privileges, int grant_option); // scope_type: 1=DATABASE, 2=SCHEMA, 3=TABLE. // privileges: comma-separated list or "ALL". int cat_find_grant(const char *username, int scope_type, const char *scope_name, GrantDesc *out); int cat_drop_grant(const char *username, int scope_type, const char *scope_name); int cat_list_grants_for_user(const char *username, GrantDesc *out, int max); // Prefix scan on "username:". int cat_drop_all_grants_for_user(const char *username); // Collects all grant keys for the user, then deletes them in a second pass // (cannot delete during a B+ tree cursor scan).

sections.heading: Convenience Three-Level Resolution
sections.text: int cat_resolve_table(const char *db_name, const char *schema_name, const char *table_name, TableDesc *out); This performs a three-step lookup. First cat_find_database. Then cat_find_schema using db.db_id. Then cat_find_table using schema.schema_id. It returns 0 on success and -1 if any level fails. This is the primary entry point used by DML operations to resolve a table name to its descriptor. Those operations are SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE. See the Table API and DML page for how DML uses this descriptor.

sections.heading: Internal Data Storage Pattern
sections.text: All catalog operations follow the same create, find, and drop pattern internally.

sections.heading: Create
sections.text: 1. Build composite B+ tree key from the input IDs and name. 2. Check for duplicates via bt2_search (return -1 if key exists). 3. Allocate an auto-increment ID via pgm_next_id(). 4. Populate the descriptor struct. 5. Serialize the struct to a semicolon-delimited string. 6. Insert the string into a slotted page via cat_store_record() → returns RowID. 7. Insert (key → RowID) into the B+ tree via bt2_insert().

sections.heading: Find
sections.text: 1. Build key. 2. bt2_search(key) → RowID. 3. cat_read_record(RowID) → semicolon-delimited string. 4. Deserialize string into the descriptor struct.

sections.heading: List with prefix scan
sections.text: 1. Build prefix (e.g., "0000000005:"). 2. bt2_cursor_seek(prefix) → position cursor at first matching key. 3. Loop: bt2_cursor_next() → (key, RowID). 4. Break when key no longer starts with the prefix. 5. Read and deserialize each record.

sections.heading: Drop
sections.text: 1. Build key. 2. bt2_search(key) → RowID. 3. cat_delete_record(RowID) marks slot as deleted on the slotted page. 4. bt2_delete(key) removes entry from the B+ tree.

sections.heading: Update with in-place or relocate
sections.text: This applies to mutable fields such as auto_inc_counter, heap_page, and root_page. 1. bt2_search(key) → RowID. 2. Read record, deserialize, modify field, re-serialize. 3. Try slot_update() for in-place replacement on the same slotted page. 4. If the updated record does not fit in the same slot: a. Delete the record from the slotted page. b. Insert the record on a (possibly different) slotted page → new RowID. c. bt2_update(key, new_RowID) to point the B+ tree at the relocated record.

sections.heading: Slotted Page Management
sections.text: Each of the 8 catalog trees keeps a current data page for new record insertions. The current page is held in g_cat_data_pages[CatalogID]. When cat_store_record is called, it does the following. If a current data page exists, it tries slot_insert on it. If the page is full, or no current page is set, it allocates a fresh PAGE_DATA page through pgm_alloc_page. It initializes the page with slot_page_init, inserts the record, and updates g_cat_data_pages. infoAfter a server restart, all g_cat_data_pages entries reset to 0. The first insert into each catalog tree allocates a fresh data page. This can waste up to 8 pages, which is 32 KB, per restart. It avoids the complexity of scanning for partially-full data pages.

sections.heading: Constants
sections.text: #define CAT_MAX_COLUMNS 64 // max columns per table #define CAT_MAX_NAME_LEN 128 // max identifier length #define CAT_MAX_DEFAULT_LEN 256 // max DEFAULT value string #define CAT_MAX_HASH_LEN 512 // max password hash length #define CAT_MAX_KEY_LEN 255 // matches BT2_MAX_KEY_LEN #define CAT_MAX_RECORD_LEN 2048 // max serialized record size

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: Table API and DML The Table API in table_api.h and table_api.c bridges SQL operations and the storage engine. It wraps catalog resolution, heap page input and output, primary key management, and full table scans. The DML modules build INSERT, SELECT, UPDATE, DELETE, DROP, CREATE, and index operations on top of it. All table data is stored in slotted pages for heap storage. Rows are indexed by B+ trees through the btree2 module. The system catalog tracks table metadata, column definitions, constraints, and index descriptors. Every DML module depends on the Table API. See also the unified storage overview, the page manager and slotted pages, and the B+ tree pages.

sections.heading: Table API
sections.text: The Table API is a focused set of functions in table_api.h and table_api.c. It encapsulates catalog resolution, heap page input and output, primary key management, and full table scanning. Every DML module depends on it.

sections.heading: tapi_resolve, catalog resolution
sections.text: int tapi_resolve(const char *name_or_path, TableDesc *td, ColumnDesc *cols, int *ncols); This function resolves a table name into a TableDesc structure and an optional ColumnDesc array. The name can be a plain name like "employees". It can also be a full path like "root/evosql/default/employees". The work happens in three steps. It strips directory prefixes and the .dat extension through tapi_basename(). It calls cat_resolve_table(g_currentDatabase, g_currentSchema, name, td) to look up the table in the system catalog. The lookup uses the current database and schema context. If cols and ncols are non-NULL, it calls cat_find_columns(td->table_id, cols, CAT_MAX_COLUMNS) to fill the column descriptors. It returns 0 on success. It returns -1 if the table does not exist.

sections.heading: tapi_pk_tree, B+ tree handle for the primary key
sections.text: static inline BTree2 tapi_pk_tree(const TableDesc *td) { BTree2 tree = { td->pk_root_page }; return tree; } This function builds a BTree2 handle from the table's pk_root_page. It is a zero-cost inline. Every DML operation uses it to reach the primary key index.

sections.heading: tapi_build_pk_key and tapi_build_pk_key_from_vals, PK key composition
sections.text: int tapi_build_pk_key(const ColumnDesc *cols, int ncols, const char *record, char *key_out, int key_size); int tapi_build_pk_key_from_vals(const ColumnDesc *cols, int ncols, char **vals, int nvals, char *key_out, int key_size); These functions build a composite primary key string from record fields. They scan the ColumnDesc array for columns with is_pk == 1 and join their values. Single PK. The key looks like "42". Composite PK. The key looks like "42|alice". The parts are pipe-delimited. If no column has is_pk set, the first column is used as a fallback. The _from_vals variant takes a pre-parsed value array. The plain variant parses a semicolon-delimited record string on its own.

sections.heading: tapi_get_pk_indices, PK column positions
sections.text: int tapi_get_pk_indices(const ColumnDesc *cols, int ncols, int *indices, int max); This function returns the ordinal positions of PK columns within the schema. UPDATE and DELETE use it to detect when a SET clause changes a PK column. A change to a PK column requires B+ tree key updates.

sections.heading: Heap page operations
sections.text: All record data lives in slotted pages. The pages are chained together through PageHeader.next_page. The Table API provides four heap operations. tapi_heap_insert RowID tapi_heap_insert(TableDesc *td, const char *record, uint16_t rec_len); It tries slot_insert() on the table's current heap_page. If the page is full, or if no heap page exists, it allocates a fresh PAGE_DATA page through pgm_alloc_page(). It initializes the page with slot_page_init(). It chains the page by setting new_page->next_page = td->heap_page. It updates td->heap_page and persists it through cat_update_heap_page(). It returns the RowID of the inserted record. The RowID is a page number and a slot index. It returns {0, 0} on error. tapi_heap_read int tapi_heap_read(RowID rid, char *buf, int bufsize); This function reads the page at rid.page_no. It then calls slot_read() for rid.slot_idx. It returns the record length, which is null-terminated, or -1 on error. tapi_heap_update RowID tapi_heap_update(TableDesc *td, RowID old_rid, const char *new_record, uint16_t rec_len); This function tries an in-place update through slot_update(). If the record does not fit in the existing slot, it falls back to delete-then-insert. The slot's free space is not enough in that case. It deletes the old slot. It then calls tapi_heap_insert() to place the record in a page with space. It returns the RowID, which may have changed. tapi_heap_delete int tapi_heap_delete(RowID rid); This function reads the page. It calls slot_delete() to mark the slot as free. It writes the page back. It returns 0 on success.

sections.heading: Full table scan
sections.text: int tapi_scan_begin(const TableDesc *td, TableScanCursor *cursor); int tapi_scan_next(TableScanCursor *cursor, char *pk_key_out, char *record_out, int rec_out_size); Table scans iterate over the PK B+ tree, not the heap chain. This yields records in primary key order. tapi_scan_begin() opens a cursor at the first leaf entry through bt2_cursor_first(). Each call to tapi_scan_next() advances the cursor with bt2_cursor_next(). It retrieves the PK key and the RowID. It then fetches the record through tapi_heap_read(). The TableScanCursor wraps a BTree2Cursor and the BTree2 handle. typedef struct { BTree2Cursor bt_cursor; BTree2 tree; } TableScanCursor;

sections.heading: tapi_free_heap_pages
sections.text: void tapi_free_heap_pages(const TableDesc *td); This function walks the heap page chain. It starts at td->heap_page and follows next_page pointers. It calls pgm_free_page() on each page. DROP TABLE and bulk cleanup use it.

sections.heading: CREATE TABLE (Create.c)
sections.text: CreateTableProcess() orchestrates table creation through the catalog and storage layers. Catalog registration. It calls cat_create_table() to register the table in CAT_SYS_TABLES. This assigns a unique table_id. PK B+ tree creation. It calls bt2_create() to allocate the root page for the primary key index. The root page number is stored in the catalog through cat_update_pk_root(). Column registration. It calls cat_create_columns() to store each column's metadata in CAT_SYS_COLUMNS. The metadata covers the name, type code, nullability, default value, uniqueness, and PK flag. Constraint registration. It registers CHECK constraints, UNIQUE constraints, and FOREIGN KEY constraints in CAT_SYS_CONSTRAINTS. AUTO_INCREMENT. If any column has AUTO_INCREMENT, the auto-increment column index, counter, and step are stored in the TableDesc and persisted in the catalog.

sections.heading: INSERT (Insert.c)
sections.text: INSERT INTO employees (name, dept, salary) VALUES ('Alice', 'Eng', 90000); Data flow. Parser (RPN) --> InsertProcess() | v tapi_resolve(tblName) -- catalog lookup | v [Column reordering] -- if INSERT specifies column list | v [AUTO_INCREMENT] -- generate value if needed | v [Validation pipeline] -- types, NOT NULL, UNIQUE, CHECK | v [Unique index pre-check] -- btree_search on secondary indexes | v store_single_row() | +-- tapi_build_pk_key() -- compose PK from values +-- bt2_search() -- duplicate PK check +-- g_tx_undo_callback() -- record TX_OP_INSERT undo entry +-- tapi_heap_insert() -- store record in slotted page +-- bt2_insert() -- index PK in B+ tree | v [Secondary index update] -- btree_insert() for each index | v [Persist AUTO_INCREMENT] -- cat_update_auto_inc() Key details. Multi-row INSERT. g_insert contains all rows separated by ROW_SEP, which is \x02. Each row is processed on its own through the validation and storage pipeline. Column reordering. When the INSERT gives a column list, such as INSERT INTO t (c2, c1) VALUES ..., reorder_row_for_column_map() maps the user-specified column order to the schema's physical column order. It fills missing columns with DEFAULT values or NULL. Duplicate PK detection. store_single_row() calls bt2_search() before inserting. If the key exists, it returns 1 for duplicate without changing any data. Root page tracking. After bt2_insert(), the function updates td->pk_root_page = pk_tree.root_page. A B+ tree split may have created a new root page.

sections.heading: SELECT (Select.c)
sections.text: SELECT * FROM employees WHERE dept = 'Eng'; Data flow. Parser (RPN) --> SelectAll() / SelectProcess() | v tapi_resolve(tblName) -- catalog lookup | v +---[Access path decision]---+ | | Full Scan PK Lookup | | v v tapi_scan_begin() bt2_search(pk_tree, key) tapi_scan_next() loop | | v v tapi_heap_read(rid) [WHERE evaluation] | | v v [Single result] [ORDER BY sort] | v [Result output] SelectAll() handles SELECT * with two paths. Simple scan. It opens a TableScanCursor through tapi_scan_begin(). It iterates with tapi_scan_next() and prints each record. ORDER BY. It collects all records into an array. It sorts them with qsort() using the specified column index g_sortColIndex and direction g_sortDesc. It then prints the sorted result. SelectProcess() lives in the adaptor's query_executor.c. It handles full SQL SELECT with WHERE. PK Lookup. When WHERE matches the primary key column with =, it performs a single bt2_search() plus tapi_heap_read(). Index Scan. When WHERE matches a secondary index column, it uses sec_idx_search() to find matching PKs. It then fetches each one through bt2_search() plus tapi_heap_read(). Sequential Scan. It falls back to tapi_scan_begin() and tapi_scan_next() over all rows. It evaluates the WHERE expression on each record through expr_evaluate().

sections.heading: UPDATE (Update.c)
sections.text: UPDATE employees SET salary = 95000 WHERE name = 'Alice'; Data flow. Parser (RPN) --> UpdateProcess() | v tapi_resolve(tblName) -- catalog lookup | v [Identify matching rows] -- WHERE scan or PK lookup | v ApplyUpdateToRow() | +-- bt2_search(pk_tree, pkKey) +-- tapi_heap_read(rid) -- fetch current record +-- g_tx_undo_callback() -- TX_OP_UPDATE with old data +-- [Modify fields] -- apply SET column = value +-- [CHECK validation] -- expr_evaluate on updated row +-- [Unique index check] -- btree_search for conflicts +-- tapi_heap_update() -- write modified record +-- bt2_update() -- update PK tree if RowID moved +-- [Secondary idx update] -- btree_delete old + btree_insert new Key details. In-place update versus relocated update. tapi_heap_update() first tries slot_update() in place. If the record grew and no longer fits, it deletes the old slot and inserts into a page with space. That returns a different RowID. When the RowID changes, bt2_update() updates the PK B+ tree's value pointer. Secondary index maintenance. The SET clause may modify an indexed column. For each secondary index on a modified column, the function deletes the old composite key with btree_delete(path, old_value, pk). It then inserts the new one with btree_insert(path, new_value, pk). CHECK constraint re-evaluation. After the field values change in memory, all CHECK constraints are re-evaluated against the updated row before it is persisted.

sections.heading: DELETE (Delete.c)
sections.text: DELETE FROM employees WHERE dept = 'Eng'; Data flow. Parser (RPN) --> DeleteProcess() | v tapi_resolve(tblName) -- catalog lookup | v +---[WHERE present?]---+ | | Expression WHERE PK-based delete | | v v tapi_scan_begin/next() bt2_search(pkKey) [expr_evaluate loop] | [collect matching keys] v | tapi_heap_read(rid) v | [Phase 2: delete] v for each match: g_tx_undo_callback() | | +-- bt2_search() v +-- tapi_heap_read() [sec idx cleanup] +-- g_tx_undo_callback() | +-- [sec idx cleanup] v +-- tapi_heap_delete() tapi_heap_delete() +-- bt2_delete() bt2_delete() Two-phase WHERE delete. Phase 1, scan and collect. It iterates all records through tapi_scan_begin() and tapi_scan_next(). It evaluates the WHERE expression on each row. It collects matching PK keys into a dynamically allocated array called matchKeys. This avoids modifying the B+ tree while iterating it. Phase 2, delete collected. For each collected key, it looks up the RowID through bt2_search(). It reads the record for undo logging and index cleanup. It removes the row from secondary indexes. It then calls tapi_heap_delete() and bt2_delete().

sections.heading: DROP TABLE (Drop.c)
sections.text: DROP TABLE employees; Data flow. Parser (RPN) --> DropTableProcess() | v tapi_resolve(tblName) -- catalog lookup | v bt2_destroy(pk_tree) -- free all PK B+ tree pages | v tapi_free_heap_pages() -- walk heap chain, pgm_free_page each | v [Destroy secondary indexes] -- cat_list_indexes + bt2_destroy each | v cat_drop_table(table_id) -- remove from catalog cat_drop_table() removes the table entry from the system catalog B+ trees. It also removes all associated column entries, all index entries, and all constraint entries. After DROP TABLE, all storage pages return to the free list. This covers PK tree nodes, heap data pages, and secondary index nodes. TRUNCATE TABLE runs through TruncateTableProcess(). It preserves the table structure but deletes all data. It collects all PK keys through a B+ tree cursor scan. For each key it calls tapi_heap_delete() and bt2_delete(). The table's PK tree and heap pages stay allocated but empty.

sections.heading: Secondary indexes (Index.c)
sections.text: Secondary indexes are B+ trees. Each key encodes both the indexed column value and the row's primary key. This ensures uniqueness at the B+ tree level.

sections.heading: Composite key format
sections.text: "indexed_value" + \x1F + "pk_value" The Unit Separator character is \x1F, ASCII 31. It is chosen for three reasons. It sorts before all printable characters. This enables efficient prefix scans. It never appears in user data. A prefix scan on "indexed_value\x1F" finds all rows that match that indexed value. For multi-column composite indexes, column values within the indexed portion are pipe-delimited. They look like "val1|val2\x1Fpk".

sections.heading: Core operations
sections.text: sec_idx_search int sec_idx_search(uint32_t root_page, const char *indexed_val, char results[][256], int max_results); This function performs a prefix scan on the secondary index B+ tree. It seeks to "indexed_val\x1F" through bt2_cursor_seek(). It then iterates forward with bt2_cursor_next() until the prefix no longer matches. It returns the PK portion of each matching key, which is the part after \x1F. A companion function sec_idx_search_rids() returns RowID values directly instead of PK strings. sec_idx_insert int sec_idx_insert(uint32_t *root_page, const char *indexed_val, const char *pk, RowID heap_rid); This function builds the composite key through sec_idx_make_key(). It inserts the key into the B+ tree with the heap RowID as the value. It updates *root_page if the tree's root splits. sec_idx_delete int sec_idx_delete(uint32_t *root_page, const char *indexed_val, const char *pk); This function builds the composite key and deletes it from the B+ tree. The PK component ensures only the exact row's entry is removed.

sections.heading: Legacy wrapper functions
sections.text: The DML modules use wrapper functions. These encode index identity as a path string in the format "table_id:index_name:root_page". btree_insert(path, key, pk). It parses the path, builds the composite key, calls bt2_insert(), and updates the catalog root page through cat_update_index_root() if it changed. btree_search(path, key, results, max). It parses the path and delegates to sec_idx_search(). btree_delete(path, key, pk). It parses the path, builds the composite key, calls bt2_delete(), and updates the catalog if the root changed. These wrappers give DML code a stable interface. They hide the composite key encoding and the catalog root-page tracking.

sections.heading: CREATE INDEX (CreateIndexProcess)
sections.text: CREATE INDEX idx_dept ON employees(dept); CREATE UNIQUE INDEX idx_email ON employees(email); Resolve table. It calls tapi_resolve() to verify the table exists and to retrieve column metadata. Verify columns. It checks that all specified columns exist in the schema. It supports composite indexes such as col1,col2. Check duplicates. It queries cat_list_indexes() to see if an index on the same columns already exists. It respects IF NOT EXISTS. Create B+ tree. It calls bt2_create() to allocate the root page for the index. Populate from existing data. It opens a TableScanCursor and iterates all rows. For each row it does the following. It extracts the indexed column values from the record. For UNIQUE indexes, it calls sec_idx_search() to check for duplicates. If a duplicate is found, it destroys the partially built tree and returns an error. It builds the composite key indexed_value\x1Fpk_value and calls bt2_insert(). Register in catalog. It calls cat_create_index() to store the index metadata. The metadata covers the name, root page, column list, and type 'U' or 'N'.

sections.heading: DROP INDEX (DropIndexProcess)
sections.text: It finds the index by name through cat_find_index_by_name(). It rejects dropping PK indexes, where index_type == 'P'. It destroys the B+ tree through bt2_destroy(). It removes the index from the catalog through cat_drop_index().

sections.heading: EXPLAIN integration
sections.text: The query executor classifies access methods for EXPLAIN output. Access methodCondition PK Lookup (Hash)WHERE clause matches the primary key column with = Index Scan (Unique B-tree)WHERE column has a UNIQUE secondary index Index Scan (B-tree)WHERE column has a non-unique secondary index Seq ScanNo applicable index. A full table scan is required

sections.heading: Transactions
sections.text: EvoSQL implements single-connection ACID transactions. It uses an undo-log approach. The transaction subsystem lives in adaptor/transaction.h and adaptor/transaction.c.

sections.heading: Data structures
sections.text: typedef struct UndoEntry { int op_type; /* TX_OP_INSERT (1) / TX_OP_UPDATE (2) / TX_OP_DELETE (3) */ char *table; /* table name */ char *key; /* primary key value */ char *old_data; /* previous record data (NULL for INSERT) */ struct UndoEntry *next; /* linked list, stack order (newest first) */ } UndoEntry; typedef struct { UndoEntry *head; /* most recent entry (top of stack) */ int count; /* number of entries */ } UndoLog; The undo log is a singly-linked list used as a stack. Entries are pushed to the head. This way rollback processes them in reverse chronological order.

sections.heading: Session integration
sections.text: Each client connection keeps a SessionCtx. It is defined in adaptor/query_executor.h. typedef struct { char database[256]; char schema[256]; char username[256]; int in_transaction; /* 1 if BEGIN was issued */ int tx_aborted; /* 1 if error occurred in transaction */ UndoLog *undo_log; /* undo entries for rollback */ } SessionCtx; Transaction state transitions. BEGIN. It sets in_transaction = 1 and allocates a fresh UndoLog. COMMIT. It sets in_transaction = 0 and frees the UndoLog. Changes are now permanent. ROLLBACK. It calls undo_log_rollback(), frees the log, and resets in_transaction = 0 and tx_aborted = 0. Error in transaction. It sets tx_aborted = 1. Later queries are rejected with SQLSTATE 25P02. ROLLBACK is the exception.

sections.heading: DML hook mechanism
sections.text: DML operations record undo entries through a function pointer callback. It is declared as g_tx_undo_callback in database.h. void (*g_tx_undo_callback)(int op_type, const char *table, const char *key, const char *data); Before each query, safe_query_execute() in server.c checks whether the session is in a transaction. In transaction. It sets g_tx_undo_callback = tx_undo_callback. It sets g_current_undo_log, which is thread-local, to the session's undo log. Not in transaction. It sets both to NULL. Each DML operation checks g_tx_undo_callback before it modifies data. OperationUndo entry INSERTTX_OP_INSERT, key = PK, old_data = NULL UPDATETX_OP_UPDATE, key = PK, old_data = pre-update record DELETETX_OP_DELETE, key = PK, old_data = deleted record After the query completes, safe_query_execute() clears the callback and the thread-local undo log pointer.

sections.heading: Rollback
sections.text: undo_log_rollback() traverses the undo log from head to tail. That is reverse chronological order. It applies the inverse operation for each entry. Original opUndo action TX_OP_INSERTbt2_search() to find the RowID, then tapi_heap_delete() and bt2_delete() TX_OP_UPDATEbt2_search() to find the RowID, then tapi_heap_update() with the old data, then bt2_update() if the RowID moved TX_OP_DELETEtapi_heap_insert() with the old data, then bt2_insert() to restore the PK index entry Each undo action resolves the table through tapi_resolve() using the stored table name. This ensures it works with the correct catalog state.

sections.heading: Disconnect auto-rollback
sections.text: Both protocol handlers check for open transactions when a client disconnects. The PG wire protocol handler is pg_handler.c. The EVO text protocol handler is evo_protocol.c. If session.in_transaction is true, the handler does the following. It allocates a temporary QueryContext. The per-query context is already freed. It restores the database and schema from the session. It calls undo_log_rollback(). It frees the undo log and resets the session state. infoThis guarantees that uncommitted changes are never persisted when a client drops its connection.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: Unified Storage Overview EvolutionDB keeps all of its data in one binary file called evosql.db. This single file holds databases, schemas, tables, indexes, users, grants, and the system catalog. The file is split into fixed-size 4KB pages. Every disk read and write goes through a Clock Sweep buffer pool. All structural links inside the file are kept with B+ tree indexes and slotted pages.

sections.heading: Architecture Overview
sections.text: All data lives in one file made of 4KB pages. Page 0 holds the file header. The rest of the file holds data pages, B+ tree nodes, and free pages. evosql.db (single binary file, 4KB pages) Page 0: FileHeader (magic, free list head, catalog roots, ID counters) Page 1..N: Data pages, B+ tree nodes, free pages The storage engine is built in layers. Each layer depends only on the layer below it. ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ DML Modules (Insert / Select / ...) │ ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ Table API │ System Catalog │ │ tapi_heap_insert │ cat_create_table │ │ tapi_scan_begin │ cat_find_columns │ │ tapi_build_pk_key │ cat_resolve_table │ ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ B+ Tree (bt2_insert / search / cursor) │ ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ Slotted Pages (slot_insert / read) │ ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ Page Manager (pgm_alloc / read / write) │ ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ Buffer Pool (bp_read / bp_write) │ └──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ ┌────────────────┐ │ evosql.db │ └────────────────┘

sections.heading: Page Layout
sections.text: Every page is exactly EVO_PAGE_SIZE bytes, which is 4096. The first 16 bytes of every page hold a common header. typedef struct { uint8_t page_type; /* PAGE_FREE=0, PAGE_DATA=1, PAGE_BTREE_INT=2, PAGE_BTREE_LEAF=3, PAGE_OVERFLOW=4 */ uint8_t flags; /* reserved */ uint16_t free_space; /* bytes of free space on this page */ uint32_t page_id; /* self-identifying page number */ uint32_t next_page; /* free list chain or leaf chain */ uint32_t prev_page; /* B+ tree leaf backward pointer */ } PageHeader; /* 16 bytes */ Usable space per page is 4096 - 16 = 4080 bytes.

sections.heading: Page 0 File Header
sections.text: Page 0 is reserved for the FileHeader. It is never freed. FieldSizeDescription magic4B0x45564F44 ("EVOD") version2BFile format version (currently 1) page_size2BAlways 4096 total_pages4BTotal pages in file free_list_head4BFirst free page (0 = none) catalog_roots[16]64BRoot page numbers for system catalog B+ trees next_table_id4BAuto-increment counter for table IDs next_schema_id4BAuto-increment counter for schema IDs next_db_id4BAuto-increment counter for database IDs reserved4004BPad to full page

sections.heading: Free List
sections.text: Free pages are chained through the next_page field in their PageHeader. When pgm_alloc_page() is called, the page manager does one of two things. If the free list is not empty, it pops the head page and initializes it with the requested type. If the free list is empty, it grows the file by 50% of the current size, with a minimum of 16 pages. It chains the new pages into the free list. Then it allocates. When pgm_free_page() is called, the page is zeroed and pushed onto the front of the free list. Page 0 cannot be freed. The initial file is 64 pages, which is 256KB. Pages 1 through 63 form the initial free list.

sections.heading: Buffer Pool
sections.text: All page I/O flows through a Clock Sweep buffer pool in buffer_pool.h/c. It is modeled after PostgreSQL's buffer manager. The pool caches 4KB pages in memory. It uses write-back semantics. Dirty pages are flushed to disk only on eviction or on an explicit flush. Key parameters. ConstantValueDescription BP_PAGE_SIZE4096Cache page granularity BP_DEFAULT_PAGES256Default pool size (1MB) BP_MAX_USAGE5Maximum usage count per page BP_RING_SIZE8Sequential scan ring buffer slots Clock Sweep algorithm. A clock hand rotates through all buffer slots. When a victim is needed, the pool checks each slot in turn. If pin_count > 0, skip the page. It is in use. If usage_count == 0, select the page as the victim. Flush it to disk if it is dirty, then evict it. Otherwise, decrement usage_count and advance the hand. Each cache hit increments usage_count, up to BP_MAX_USAGE. This gives frequently accessed pages more chances to survive eviction sweeps. Anti-pollution ring buffer. Sequential scans use bp_read_seq() with a private BPRing. The ring has 8 slots and works round-robin. This stops large table scans from evicting hot pages from the main pool. Core API. void bp_init(int num_pages); /* allocate pool */ int bp_read(int fd, void *buf, size_t count, off_t offset); int bp_write(int fd, const void *buf, size_t count, off_t offset); void bp_flush_all(void); /* write all dirty pages to disk */ void bp_flush_fd(int fd); /* flush dirty pages for one fd */ void bp_invalidate_fd(int fd); /* flush + drop all pages for fd */ The pool uses an open-addressing hash table for fast lookup. The table is 4 times the pool size and uses linear probing. It is keyed by (fd, page_no) for O(1) page lookup. An internal mutex serializes all pool operations for thread safety.

sections.heading: Page Manager (page_mgr.h/c)
sections.text: The page manager manages the global evosql.db file. It provides page allocation and deallocation, free list management, file growth, and access to file header fields. The header fields include the catalog root pages and the auto-increment counters. All reads and writes delegate to the buffer pool. See Page Manager and Slotted Pages.

sections.heading: Slotted Pages (slotted.h/c)
sections.text: Slotted pages store variable-length records within a single 4KB data page. The page layout is shown below. [PageHeader 16B][SlottedHeader 6B][Slot Directory -->] [ FREE SPACE ] [<-- Records packed from end of page] Each record is identified by a RowID = {page_no, slot_idx}, which is 6 bytes. A RowID with page_no == 0 is the "deleted" sentinel. This works because page 0 is always the file header. The slot directory grows forward. Records grow backward. Deleted slots can be reused. slot_compact() defragments records to reclaim space from deletions. Maximum record size is 4096 - 16 - 6 - 4 = 4070 bytes. See Page Manager and Slotted Pages.

sections.heading: B+ Tree (btree2.h/c)
sections.text: The B+ tree provides O(log n) key-to-RowID indexing. Each B+ tree node occupies one 4KB page. Leaf nodes are doubly linked for efficient sequential and range scans. Key format. The key is a length-prefixed string. It uses 1 byte for the length followed by up to 255 bytes of data. Key comparison is numeric-aware. If both keys parse as numbers, they are compared as double. Otherwise lexicographic comparison is used. Leaf entry. [key_len:1][key_data:N][RowID:6] Internal entry. [child0:4][key0_len:1][key0:N][child1:4]... Insertions handle splits automatically. They propagate separator keys up to the parent and create a new root when needed. Deletions use lazy marking. They set the RowID to {0, 0}. Cursor operations traverse the leaf chain for ordered iteration. These are bt2_cursor_first, bt2_cursor_next, and bt2_cursor_seek. See B+ Tree.

sections.heading: System Catalog (catalog_internal.h/c)
sections.text: The system catalog stores all metadata in 8 B+ trees. There is one tree per catalog table. CatalogIDTreeKey FormatDescription 0CAT_SYS_DATABASESdb_nameDatabase registry 1CAT_SYS_SCHEMASdb_id:schema_nameSchemas per database 2CAT_SYS_TABLESschema_id:table_nameTables per schema 3CAT_SYS_COLUMNStable_id:col_ordinalColumn definitions 4CAT_SYS_INDEXEStable_id:index_nameIndex metadata 5CAT_SYS_CONSTRAINTStable_id:ordinalCHECK, UNIQUE, PK, FK 6CAT_SYS_USERSusernameUser credentials 7CAT_SYS_GRANTSusername:scope_type:scope_namePrivilege grants Composite keys use zero-padded 10-digit IDs with a : separator. An example is "0000000001:tablename". Catalog records are stored as semicolon-delimited strings on slotted pages. The B+ tree entries point to those records. Root page numbers for all 8 trees are persisted in the file header's catalog_roots[] array. See System Catalog.

sections.heading: Table API (table_api.h/c)
sections.text: The Table API bridges the DML modules and the storage layers. It provides the following operations. Table resolution tapi_resolve() looks up a table by name using the catalog. It returns a TableDesc and arrays of ColumnDesc. Heap operations tapi_heap_insert(), tapi_heap_read(), tapi_heap_delete(), and tapi_heap_update() manage records on slotted pages. Pages are chained through next_page in the page header. New pages are allocated when existing ones are full. PK key building tapi_build_pk_key() extracts the primary key columns from a semicolon-delimited record. It builds the B+ tree key string. A single column gives "value". A composite key gives "val1|val2". Table scan tapi_scan_begin() and tapi_scan_next() iterate all records ordered by primary key. They use the PK B+ tree cursor. Each cursor step reads the RowID from the B+ tree. Then it fetches the full record from the heap page. Cleanup tapi_free_heap_pages() walks the heap chain and frees all pages. This is used by DROP TABLE. See Table API and DML.

sections.heading: INSERT
sections.text: SQL: INSERT INTO employees (id, name) VALUES (1, 'Alice'); evoparser.y (parse + emit RPN) | Insert.c InsertProcess() | +-- tapi_resolve("employees") --> TableDesc, ColumnDesc[] | +-- tapi_build_pk_key(cols, record) --> "1" | +-- bt2_search(pk_tree, "1") --> check for duplicate PK | +-- tapi_heap_insert(td, "1;Alice") --> RowID{page=5, slot=3} | | | +-- pgm_read_page(heap_page) (via buffer pool) | +-- slot_insert(page_buf, record) | +-- pgm_write_page(heap_page) (via buffer pool) | +-- bt2_insert(pk_tree, "1", RowID{5,3}) | +-- find_leaf() --> descend internal nodes +-- write entry to leaf page (split if full) +-- pgm_write_page() (via buffer pool)

sections.heading: SELECT (full scan)
sections.text: SQL: SELECT * FROM employees; Select.c SelectProcess() | +-- tapi_resolve("employees") --> TableDesc, ColumnDesc[] | +-- tapi_scan_begin(td, &cursor) --> positions at first PK | +-- loop: tapi_scan_next(&cursor, pk_key, record_buf) | +-- bt2_cursor_next() --> RowID +-- tapi_heap_read(RowID) --> "1;Alice" +-- apply WHERE filter (expression.c) +-- emit row to result set

sections.heading: SELECT (PK lookup)
sections.text: SQL: SELECT * FROM employees WHERE id = 1; Select.c SelectProcess() | +-- tapi_resolve("employees") +-- tapi_build_pk_key(cols, where_val) --> "1" +-- bt2_search(pk_tree, "1") --> RowID{5,3} +-- tapi_heap_read(RowID{5,3}) --> "1;Alice"

sections.heading: DELETE
sections.text: Delete.c DeleteProcess() | +-- tapi_scan_begin/next --> find matching rows +-- bt2_delete(pk_tree, pk_key) --> lazy-mark RowID as {0,0} +-- tapi_heap_delete(RowID) --> slot_delete on page

sections.heading: UPDATE
sections.text: Update.c UpdateProcess() | +-- tapi_scan_begin/next --> find matching rows +-- tapi_heap_update(td, old_rid, new_record) | | | +-- slot_update() if fits in-place --> done | +-- otherwise: slot_delete() + tapi_heap_insert() --> new RowID | +-- if RowID changed: bt2_update(pk_tree, pk_key, new_rid)

sections.heading: Initialization
sections.text: Server startup calls db_ensure_root() in DatabaseMgmt.c. This triggers the full initialization sequence. db_ensure_root() | +-- mkdir("root/") create data directory | +-- pgm_init("root/evosql.db") open or create the global file | | | +-- (if file exists) validate magic + page_size, load header | +-- (if file absent) create file, write FileHeader to page 0, | chain pages 1..63 into free list (256KB initial) | +-- cat_init() initialize system catalog | | | +-- (first run) create 8 B+ trees, store roots in file header, | | insert default database ("evosql") and schema ("default") | +-- (subsequent runs) load tree roots from file header | +-- db_ensure_users() create admin user if absent | | | +-- cat_find_user("admin") | +-- if not found: cat_create_user("admin", hashed_password) | +-- db_ensure_grants() create admin superuser grant | +-- cat_find_grant("admin", DATABASE, "*") +-- if not found: cat_create_grant("admin", DATABASE, "*", "ALL", 1) warningThe buffer pool must be initialized before pgm_init(). The call is bp_init(). All page I/O depends on the buffer pool. The adaptor's server.c calls bp_init() during server startup before it invokes db_ensure_root().

sections.heading: Building and Testing
sections.text: Standalone C tests validate each layer independently. # Page Manager + Slotted Pages (18 tests) gcc -Wall -g -I../evolution/db -o test_page_mgr test_page_mgr.c \ ../evolution/db/page_mgr.c ../evolution/db/slotted.c \ ../evolution/db/buffer_pool.c ../evolution/db/error.c \ ../evolution/db/database_globals.c ../evolution/db/query_context.c -lpthread # B+ Tree (13 tests) gcc -Wall -g -I../evolution/db -o test_btree2 test_btree2.c \ ../evolution/db/btree2.c ../evolution/db/page_mgr.c \ ../evolution/db/slotted.c ../evolution/db/buffer_pool.c \ ../evolution/db/error.c ../evolution/db/database_globals.c \ ../evolution/db/query_context.c -lpthread # System Catalog (30 tests) gcc -Wall -g -I../evolution/db -o test_catalog test_catalog.c \ ../evolution/db/catalog_internal.c ../evolution/db/btree2.c \ ../evolution/db/page_mgr.c ../evolution/db/slotted.c \ ../evolution/db/buffer_pool.c ../evolution/db/error.c \ ../evolution/db/database_globals.c ../evolution/db/query_context.c -lpthread Integration tests use the Python test suite against a running server. See the project README for details.
